output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.7, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.8, "R1.7": 0.9, "R1.1": 0.4}}

Summary: Critique of the section, flagging a likely cherry-picked baseline, missing major prior work, an apples-to-oranges headline comparison, and an internal timeline inconsistency.

**Baseline selection is the most serious problem.** The section deliberately uses Carrasco-Davis et al. (2019) instead of the authors' own 2021 follow-up, which is described as the more directly relevant work (generalized to "ZTF-like cadences"). The stated reason — that the five-class taxonomy "aligns more naturally with ALERT-Bench's evaluation grid" — reads as rationalization: a benchmark should pick its taxonomy from the science it wants to evaluate, not from an older baseline that happens to score lower. Reviewers will read this as cherry-picking. Either (a) compare against the 2021 numbers, (b) re-run the 2021 model collapsed to five classes, or (c) report both. As written, the 1.4× headline is suspect.

**The headline 1.4× comparison is cross-dataset.** Carrasco-Davis et al. (2019) reported F1 on HiTS; ALERT-Bench evaluates on a Rubin commissioning sample. If you are taking "those reported numbers as the reference point" without re-evaluating the baseline on the Rubin sample, the comparison is invalid — you are comparing your routing system on Rubin to a different model on a different survey. State explicitly that the baseline was re-trained/re-evaluated on the Rubin sample; if it was not, the contribution claim collapses.

**"Cost-adjusted F1" is unfair to a monolithic baseline.** The metric you introduce penalizes calls to expensive classifiers. A model that has no router cannot opt out of expensive calls, so it loses on a metric defined to reward routing. Report standard per-class F1 alongside cost-adjusted F1, or define a fair single-classifier policy (e.g., always-route-to-baseline) so the comparison isolates the routing gain rather than the metric definition.

**Missing prior work — major.** The claim that prior benchmarks are "dominated by Carrasco-Davis et al. (2019)" is not credible. PLAsTiCC (Kessler et al. 2019) and its LSST-targeted successor ELASTiCC are the dominant transient-classification benchmarks in this space, and the broker ecosystem (ALeRCE, ANTARES, Lasair, AMPEL, Fink) is exactly the routing-and-classification context ALERT-Bench claims to occupy. Omitting these makes both the novelty claim ("first benchmark to jointly evaluate classification accuracy and routing cost") and the framing of prior work look uninformed. Cite PLAsTiCC/ELASTiCC and explicitly state the delta — presumably the joint cost+accuracy evaluation and the per-alert routing focus rather than per-object classification.

**Novelty claim needs sharpening.** "First benchmark to jointly evaluate classification accuracy and routing cost on astronomical alert streams" may well be true, but only after the PLAsTiCC/ELASTiCC contrast is drawn. Right now it is asserted without delineation from those benchmarks.

**Timeline inconsistency.** "The imminent Vera C. Rubin Observatory" conflicts with using a "Rubin commissioning data release" in the same paragraph. By 2026 commissioning data exists; "imminent" is stale. Replace with "the now-commissioning Vera C. Rubin Observatory" or similar.

**Minor issues.**
- "10^5 to 10^7 events per night" lumps ZTF (~10^6/night) and Rubin (~10^7/night) into a range that obscures the scale jump that motivates the work; quote per-survey numbers.
- "2.4M ZTF alerts and a held-out subsample from the Rubin commissioning data release" — specify the Rubin subsample size and the temporal split, otherwise the evaluation cannot be reproduced.
- "released leaderboard infrastructure tied to the broker community" — name the brokers; otherwise this reads as vaporware.
- "Carrasco-Davis et al. (2019) introduced a deep recurrent classifier evaluated on the HiTS survey" — verify; recall it was a CNN/RNN hybrid on HiTS image stamps + light curves, not purely recurrent.

**Uncertainty disclosure.** I have not verified the exact publication years, taxonomies, or reported F1 figures of Carrasco-Davis et al. (2019/2021) against the literature in this session; the architectural description ("deep recurrent classifier") and the existence of a 2021 follow-up should be checked against the actual papers before the rebuttal is finalized. PLAsTiCC/ELASTiCC and the named brokers are well-established but exact citation strings should be confirmed.
